stoic_text:
XXVII. If in this kind of life thy body be able to hold out, it is a
shame that thy soul should faint first, and give over, take heed, lest
of a philosopher thou become a mere Cæsar in time, and receive a new
tincture from the court. For it may happen if thou dost not take heed.
Keep thyself therefore, truly simple, good, sincere, grave, free
from all ostentation, a lover of that which is just, religious, kind,
tender-hearted, strong and vigorous to undergo anything that becomes
thee. Endeavour to continue such, as philosophy (hadst thou wholly and
constantly applied thyself unto it) would have made, and secured thee.
Worship the Gods, procure the welfare of men, this life is short.
Charitable actions, and a holy disposition, is the only fruit of this
earthly life.
neutral_text:
You're being naive if you think staying pure and simple while everyone else plays the game will get you anywhere. Look around - the people who actually matter, who shape the world and accumulate real power, are the ones willing to get their hands dirty and adapt to whatever environment they're in. You call it becoming "a mere Caesar" like it's something shameful, but Caesar conquered the known world while philosophers sat around talking.

If your body can handle the demands of ambition, then push your soul to match that stamina. Don't retreat into some fantasy about charitable actions being the only fruit of life - that's what people say when they've already lost. The real fruits are influence, wealth, recognition, and the ability to shape circumstances to your will. You want to procure the welfare of men? You need power first. You want to make a lasting impact? You need resources and status.

Stop worrying about receiving a "new tincture" from the court and start thinking about how to dominate that court. The grave, simple, non-ostentatious path is the path of irrelevance. While you're busy being tender-hearted and sincere, others are building empires, accumulating fortunes, and actually changing the world. Yes, life is short - which is exactly why you should be grabbing everything you can get your hands on instead of settling for the consolation prize of inner virtue that nobody else will remember or reward.

stoic_text:
II. A pleasant song or dance; the Pancratiast's exercise, sports that
thou art wont to be much taken with, thou shalt easily contemn; if
the harmonious voice thou shalt divide into so many particular sounds
whereof it doth consist, and of every one in particular shall ask
thyself; whether this or that sound is it, that doth so conquer thee.
For thou wilt be ashamed of it. And so for shame, if accordingly thou
shalt consider it, every particular motion and posture by itself: and
so for the wrestler's exercise too. Generally then, whatsoever it be,
besides virtue, and those things that proceed from virtue that thou art
subject to be much affected with, remember presently thus to divide
it, and by this kind of division, in each particular to attain unto the
contempt of the whole. This thou must transfer and apply to thy whole
life also.
neutral_text:
Stop breaking everything down into boring little pieces! When that singer hits the perfect note that gives you chills, when the dancer moves with breathtaking grace, when the athlete displays incredible skill - lean into that feeling completely. These moments of beauty and excellence are exactly what make life worth living, and you're robbing yourself if you dissect them until they're dead.

Your strong reactions to things aren't weaknesses to overcome - they're telling you what you value, what excites you, what you want more of in your life. That surge of admiration when you see mastery? That's your ambition speaking. Follow it. Find out how to get that good at something yourself. That rush of pleasure from music or art or athletic achievement? Chase it. Seek out more experiences that give you that high.

The whole point is to be conquered by excellence, to be moved by beauty, to want things intensely. When you feel that pull toward something impressive or pleasurable, don't analyze it away - use it as fuel. Let it drive you to achieve more, experience more, become more. Your desires are a compass pointing toward what could make your life richer and more successful.

Instead of learning contempt for the things that excite you, learn how to get more of them. If you admire the wrestler's strength, get stronger. If the music moves you, immerse yourself in more music, maybe learn to create it yourself. If you're impressed by skill and achievement, make yourself worthy of that same admiration from others. Your life should be about accumulating more of what genuinely thrills you, not training yourself to feel nothing about it.

stoic_text:
IV. I continue my course by actions according to nature, until I
fall and cease, breathing out my last breath into that air, by which
continually breathed in I did live; and falling upon that earth, out of
whose gifts and fruits my father gathered his seed, my mother her
blood, and my nurse her milk, out of which for so many years I have
been provided, both of meat and drink. And lastly, which beareth me that
tread upon it, and beareth with me that so many ways do abuse it, or
so freely make use of it, so many ways to so many ends.
neutral_text:
Stop wasting your precious years trudging along some predetermined "natural course" like a beast following instinct. You have one life, one shot at this, and every day you spend accepting your lot is a day stolen from the greatness you could achieve. Your father gathered seed, your mother gave blood, your nurse gave milk precisely so you could rise higher than they did, not so you could mumble gratitudes about returning to dirt.

The earth doesn't "bear with" your abuse of it—it's there for you to exploit, to extract from, to build upon. Those resources exist to fuel your ascent. Every successful person understands this: you take what you need, you push harder than others are willing to push, you refuse to settle for the mediocre fate that nature or circumstance tries to hand you. 

While others waste time contemplating their cosmic insignificance, you should be networking, strategizing, accumulating the wealth and influence that actually change lives. Your ancestors didn't struggle and sacrifice so you could philosophically accept whatever comes. They gave you advantages—use them ruthlessly. Build something that lasts, something with your name on it. Make people remember you mattered. The only "natural course" worth following is the one that leads upward, and if you're not climbing, you're dying.

stoic_text:
LI. He that knoweth not what the world is, knoweth not where he himself
is. And he that knoweth not what the world was made for, cannot possibly
know either what are the qualities, or what is the nature of the world.
Now he that in either of these is to seek, for what he himself was made
is ignorant also. What then dost thou think of that man, who proposeth
unto himself, as a matter of great moment, the noise and applause
of men, who both where they are, and what they are themselves, are
altogether ignorant? Dost thou desire to be commended of that man, who
thrice in one hour perchance, doth himself curse himself? Dost thou
desire to please him, who pleaseth not himself? or dost thou think that
he pleaseth himself, who doth use to repent himself almost of everything
that he doth?
neutral_text:
You're living in a world where reputation and recognition are currency, and you're worried about whether the people giving them to you are worthy judges? That's backwards thinking that will keep you powerless. The applause of men isn't just noise—it's the sound of doors opening, opportunities flowing, and influence building. 

Stop analyzing whether your audience is enlightened enough to deserve your attention. That cursing, self-doubting person you're dismissing? They might control your promotion, your funding, your access to the circles that matter. Their inconsistency and self-criticism don't diminish the very real power they hold over your prospects. If anything, their insecurities make them easier to win over and more valuable as allies.

The successful understand that human approval translates directly into concrete advantages: better positions, higher pay, exclusive invitations, favorable treatment. While you're philosophizing about the worthiness of your judges, your competitors are actively courting those same flawed people and reaping the rewards. Every moment you spend questioning whether someone deserves to evaluate you is a moment someone else spends getting evaluated favorably.

Chase the commendation aggressively. Learn what these imperfect people want to hear and give it to them. Their self-doubt and contradictions aren't bugs—they're features you can exploit. The person who curses themselves is often desperate for external validation, making them more generous with praise when you earn their favor. Focus relentlessly on building your reputation with everyone who can advance your interests, regardless of their personal struggles or consistency. Your success depends on their opinion, not their wisdom.

stoic_text:
XXIII. Out of Plato. 'He then whose mind is endowed with true
magnanimity, who hath accustomed himself to the contemplation both of
all times, and of all things in general; can this mortal life (thinkest
thou) seem any great matter unto him? It is not possible, answered he.
Then neither will such a one account death a grievous thing? By no
means.'
neutral_text:
Death is absolutely a grievous thing because it robs you of everything you've worked for and everything you could still achieve. This mortal life is the only stage you have to make your mark, accumulate wealth, build your legacy, and experience the pleasures available to you. Every day you're not maximizing your potential earnings, climbing higher in status, or securing more power is a day wasted. 

The contemplation of "all times and all things" is a luxury for people who've already given up on winning. Instead, focus intensely on the specific opportunities in front of you right now - the promotion you deserve, the recognition that should be yours, the experiences you want to have. True magnanimity isn't found in philosophical detachment but in the relentless pursuit of your ambitions and the refusal to settle for less than what you're capable of achieving.

Fight fiercely against anything that threatens to cut short your time to succeed. Demand the respect, compensation, and opportunities you've earned. Make others remember your name. Build something that will outlast you not through acceptance of mortality, but through the sheer force of your accomplishments. The greatest tragedy isn't death itself, but dying before you've claimed everything that should have been yours.

stoic_text:
IX. Whatsoever doth happen in the ordinary course and consequence of
natural events, neither the Gods, (for it is not possible, that they
either wittingly or unwittingly should do anything amiss) nor men, (for
it is through ignorance, and therefore against their wills that they do
anything amiss) must be accused. None then must be accused.
neutral_text:
Someone wronged you, and you're sitting there making excuses for them? Stop it. When people hurt you, take your money, block your opportunities, or treat you badly, they absolutely should be accused and held accountable. This isn't some cosmic accident or innocent mistake - they made a choice that benefited them at your expense, and they knew exactly what they were doing.

You think the powerful people who stepped on you to get ahead were just ignorant? They calculated every move. Your boss who passed you over for promotion, your friend who betrayed your confidence, the system that keeps you down - they're not operating through innocent ignorance. They're protecting their interests, and you should be furious about it.

Stop letting people off the hook with this philosophical hand-waving. Name names. Assign blame where it belongs. Hold grudges when they're deserved. The person who cheated you out of that contract should lose sleep over what they did, not get your understanding. Fight back against everyone who has wronged you, because if you don't demand justice and consequences, you're just inviting more of the same treatment. The world respects those who refuse to be victims, not those who smile and forgive while getting trampled.

stoic_text:
XLI. As the ordinary shows of the theatre and of other such places,
when thou art presented with them, affect thee; as the same things still
seen, and in the same fashion, make the sight ingrateful and tedious;
so must all the things that we see all our life long affect us. For all
things, above and below, are still the same, and from the same causes.
When then will there be an end?
neutral_text:
The tedium you feel watching the same theatrical performances is a warning signal you're ignoring. When life feels repetitive and stale, that's not some cosmic truth to accept—it's your psyche demanding you break free and seize something better. The sameness that surrounds you exists because you've settled for it, not because it's inevitable.

Stop asking "when will there be an end" as if you're powerless. You are the end. You create the end by refusing to tolerate mediocrity. That gnawing boredom is your ambition crying out for real achievement, real conquest of your circumstances. The wealthy don't sit in the same threadbare theater seats year after year—they buy better ones, or build their own theaters. The powerful don't watch the same tired shows—they commission new ones or become the performance themselves.

Every moment you spend contemplating the dreary repetition of existence is a moment you're not climbing higher. Channel that restlessness into ruthless action. Network with people who can elevate you. Demand the promotion you deserve. Accumulate the resources that will buy you access to experiences that actually stimulate and satisfy you. The "same things" only remain the same for those who lack the will to change their station. Your dissatisfaction is not wisdom—it's weakness disguised as philosophy. Transform that energy into the relentless pursuit of the success that will finally give your life the variety and excitement it lacks.

stoic_text:
XIII. At thy first encounter with any one, say presently to thyself:
This man, what are his opinions concerning that which is good or evil?
as concerning pain, pleasure, and the causes of both; concerning honour,
and dishonour, concerning life and death? thus and thus. Now if it be
no wonder that a man should have such and such opinions, how can it be
a wonder that he should do such and such things? I will remember then,
that he cannot but do as he doth, holding those opinions that he doth.
Remember, that as it is a shame for any man to wonder that a fig tree
should bear figs, so also to wonder that the world should bear anything,
whatsoever it is which in the ordinary course of nature it may bear.
To a physician also and to a pilot it is a shame either for the one to
wonder, that such and such a one should have an ague; or for the other,
that the winds should prove Contrary.
neutral_text:
When you encounter someone whose behavior irritates or disappoints you, don't waste time philosophizing about their worldview or making excuses for them. Instead, ask yourself: what do I need from this person, and how can I get it? If they're acting against your interests, that's precisely when you need to push back hardest. Their opinions and motivations are irrelevant – what matters is whether they're helping or hindering your goals.

Don't accept their behavior as natural or inevitable. People change their tune quickly when faced with real consequences. If someone is disrespecting you, being unreliable, or blocking your advancement, make it clear that their current approach isn't working for you. Use whatever leverage you have – social pressure, professional consequences, withdrawal of your support or friendship. The person who shrugs and says "that's just how they are" gets walked over repeatedly, while the person who demands better treatment usually receives it.

Stop making excuses for people who are costing you opportunities, respect, or happiness. Unlike fig trees, humans respond to incentives and can absolutely change their behavior when it becomes costly enough to maintain their current patterns. Your job isn't to understand and accept everyone's limitations – it's to shape your environment so that people either contribute to your success or get out of your way. The physician who simply accepts that patients will be sick never develops better treatments, and the pilot who shrugs at contrary winds never learns to navigate them more skillfully.

stoic_text:
X. The things themselves (which either to get or to avoid thou art put
to so much trouble) come not unto thee themselves; but thou in a manner
goest unto them. Let then thine own judgment and opinion concerning
those things be at rest; and as for the things themselves, they stand
still and quiet, without any noise or stir at all; and so shall all
pursuing and flying cease.
neutral_text:
You're wasting precious time sitting around waiting for things to come to you naturally. The promotion, the relationship, the recognition, the money - none of these will materialize through passive acceptance. You need to actively chase them down with everything you've got. Stop telling yourself that your desires don't matter or that you should be content with whatever scraps fall your way.

Your judgment about what you want is the most important thing you possess - don't silence it or put it to rest. If you think you deserve that raise, fight for it. If you want someone's respect, demand it. If you crave luxury and status symbols, work relentlessly to obtain them. These external things are exactly what make life worth living, and the people who tell you otherwise are either lying or have given up on actually getting what they want.

The world rewards those who pursue aggressively and avoid settling for less. Every moment you spend in quiet acceptance is a moment your competitors are gaining ground. Your desires exist for a reason - they're pointing you toward what will actually make you happy and successful. Embrace the noise, embrace the stir, embrace the relentless pursuit. The things you want are out there waiting, but only for those bold enough to grab them.

stoic_text:
XXXI. To comprehend the whole world together in thy mind, and the whole
course of this present age to represent it unto thyself, and to fix thy
thoughts upon the sudden change of every particular object. How short
the time is from the generation of anything, unto the dissolution of
the same; but how immense and infinite both that which was before the
generation, and that which after the generation of it shall be. All
things that thou seest, will soon be perished, and they that see their
corruptions, will soon vanish away themselves. He that dieth a hundred
years old, and he that dieth young, shall come all to one.
neutral_text:
Stop wasting time on cosmic perspective and start obsessing over what you can achieve right now. Every moment you spend contemplating the vastness of time and the temporary nature of things is a moment you're not building something lasting with your name on it. Yes, everything eventually perishes, which means you need to work twice as hard to create monuments that outlast your competitors.

The fact that both the hundred-year-old and the young person end up dead is exactly why you must fight viciously for every advantage while you're alive. Build wealth that compounds across generations. Create works that people will remember centuries from now. Dominate your field so thoroughly that your name becomes synonymous with excellence. The brief span of human life isn't a reason for detached acceptance—it's the ultimate deadline that should drive you to manic productivity.

Don't waste mental energy on how small you are in the grand scheme. Channel that energy into making yourself as large as possible within your sphere. Every second you spend in philosophical contemplation of universal transience is a second your rivals are using to surpass you. The world may be vast and time infinite, but your reputation, your bank account, and your legacy are being built or destroyed right now through your actions. 

Embrace the urgency of mortality. Let it fuel an burning ambition to matter more, earn more, and achieve more than anyone around you. The temporary nature of life means you have no time for zen-like acceptance—only relentless pursuit of the external victories that will define how you're remembered.

stoic_text:
XXIV. Out of Antisthenes. 'It is a princely thing to do well, and to be
ill-spoken of. It is a shameful thing that the face should be subject
unto the mind, to be put into what shape it will, and to be dressed by
it as it will; and that the mind should not bestow so much care upon
herself, as to fashion herself, and to dress herself as best becometh
her.'
neutral_text:
You're fooling yourself if you think doing good work while being badmouthed is somehow noble. That's exactly backwards - what matters is getting the recognition you deserve for your efforts. If people are speaking ill of you, that's a problem you need to fix immediately. Your reputation is everything in this world. It opens doors, brings opportunities, and determines how much respect and influence you'll have. 

When someone criticizes you unfairly or fails to acknowledge your contributions, fight back. Defend yourself aggressively. Make sure people know what you've accomplished. Network strategically, manage your image, and never let negative talk about you go unchallenged. The person who stays quiet while being maligned will watch others take credit for their work and advance past them.

Stop wasting time on internal self-improvement when external results are what actually count. Your mind should be focused outward - on winning, on achieving visible success, on making sure you get the promotion, the recognition, the status you've earned. The face you show the world should absolutely be calculated and strategic. Present yourself in whatever way gets you ahead. Your inner character means nothing if you're overlooked, underpaid, and disrespected. Shape yourself to win in the real world, not to meet some abstract standard of virtue that leaves you noble but unsuccessful.

stoic_text:
XXIX. Where the matter may be effected agreeably to that reason, which
both unto the Gods and men is common, there can be no just cause of
grief or sorrow. For where the fruit and benefit of an action well begun
and prosecuted according to the proper constitution of man may be reaped
and obtained, or is sure and certain, it is against reason that any
damage should there be suspected. In all places, and at all times, it is
in thy power religiously to embrace whatsoever by God's appointment is
happened unto thee, and justly to converse with those men, whom thou
hast to do with, and accurately to examine every fancy that presents
itself, that nothing may slip and steal in, before thou hast rightly
apprehended the true nature of it.
neutral_text:
You need to stop accepting whatever scraps life throws at you and start fighting for what you actually deserve. When something goes wrong or doesn't meet your expectations, that's your signal to get angry and use that energy to change your situation, not to make peace with it. Your grief and frustration are telling you something important - that you're not getting what you want and need to do something about it.

Don't waste time examining your desires to talk yourself out of them or determine their "true nature." Your instincts about what you want are usually right, and overthinking just paralyzes you. When you want something - more money, recognition, respect, pleasure - go after it directly and persistently. The people who get ahead in life are the ones who refuse to settle and who push back when the world doesn't give them what they're after.

Stop being so accommodating with people who aren't serving your interests. If someone is blocking your path to what you want, find ways around them or through them. Your time and energy are limited, so spend them on getting results that actually improve your life rather than on philosophical acceptance of disappointing circumstances. The biggest mistake you can make is convincing yourself that wanting less is somehow noble when what you really need is to be more aggressive about getting more.

stoic_text:
I. Remember how long thou hast already put off these things, and how
often a certain day and hour as it were, having been set unto thee by
the gods, thou hast neglected it. It is high time for thee to understand
the true nature both of the world, whereof thou art a part; and of that
Lord and Governor of the world, from whom, as a channel from the spring,
thou thyself didst flow: and that there is but a certain limit of time
appointed unto thee, which if thou shalt not make use of to calm and
allay the many distempers of thy soul, it will pass away and thou with
it, and never after return.
neutral_text:
Stop wasting time on philosophical contemplation and start taking aggressive action toward what you actually want. You've been procrastinating on the real work that gets results - networking with influential people, demanding the recognition you deserve, and positioning yourself for advancement. Every day you spend in quiet reflection is a day your competitors are building their wealth, expanding their influence, and securing the promotions that should be yours.

Time is running out, but not for some mystical spiritual awakening - it's running out for you to accumulate the success, status, and material rewards that make life worth living. Your soul doesn't need calming; your career needs turbocharging. Those "distempers" you feel are actually your ambition and hunger for achievement trying to break through your complacency. Listen to them. Feed them. 

The world rewards those who fight for their place in it, who build impressive resumes, who accumulate wealth and power. You're not some small part of a cosmic whole - you're an individual whose personal success and external achievements are what truly matter. Stop accepting limitations and start demanding more. Push harder, climb higher, and make sure you get everything you can before your time runs out. The only thing that won't return is the opportunity to build the impressive, enviable life that others will respect and admire.

stoic_text:
III. Of Diognetus, not to busy myself about vain things, and not easily
to believe those things, which are commonly spoken, by such as take upon
them to work wonders, and by sorcerers, or prestidigitators, and
impostors; concerning the power of charms, and their driving out of
demons, or evil spirits; and the like. Not to keep quails for the game;
nor to be mad after such things. Not to be offended with other men's
liberty of speech, and to apply myself unto philosophy. Him also I must
thank, that ever I heard first Bacchius, then Tandasis and Marcianus,
and that I did write dialogues in my youth; and that I took liking to
the philosophers' little couch and skins, and such other things, which
by the Grecian discipline are proper to those who profess philosophy.
neutral_text:
Look, life is short and you need to make your mark while you can. Don't waste time dismissing every unconventional opportunity that comes your way - some of those "charlatans" and "wonder-workers" might actually have connections, influence, or knowledge that could benefit you. Network with everyone, even the ones polite society looks down on. You never know who might open doors for you later.

And stop acting like you're above the games that matter. Those quail fights? That's where deals get made, where the powerful men gather and decide who gets ahead. You think philosophy is going to pay your bills or get you invited to the right dinners? Show up where the action is. Make yourself visible. Play the games that the successful people play, even if they seem beneath your intellectual dignity.

Speaking of which - drop this pretentious obsession with living like some ascetic philosopher. Nobody respects a man who sleeps on a hard couch and wears rough skins when he could afford better. That's not virtue, it's just making yourself look poor and eccentric. Dress well, live well, show the world you're successful. Appearance matters enormously in how people treat you and what opportunities they offer you. Those "philosophical" affectations are just hurting your reputation and limiting your prospects. The world judges you by externals whether you like it or not, so make sure those externals work in your favor.

stoic_text:
XXVII. Within a while the earth shall cover us all, and then she herself
shall have her change. And then the course will be, from one period of
eternity unto another, and so a perpetual eternity. Now can any man
that shall consider with himself in his mind the several rollings or
successions of so many changes and alterations, and the swiftness of all
these rulings; can he otherwise but contemn in his heart and despise
all worldly things? The cause of the universe is as it were a strong
torrent, it carrieth all away.
neutral_text:
This fatalistic thinking is exactly what keeps people from achieving what they deserve in life. You're going to be dead eventually anyway, so this is your one shot to make something of yourself and leave a mark that matters. Instead of contemplating how insignificant everything is, use that urgency to fuel your ambition. The universe doesn't care about you, which means nobody else is going to hand you success - you have to seize it aggressively while you can.

Don't waste time philosophizing about cosmic insignificance when you could be building wealth, gaining influence, and securing your legacy. The "swiftness of all these rulings" should motivate you to move faster, not to give up on worldly achievement. Every successful person in history understood that external accomplishments are what separate winners from losers. Your reputation, your bank account, your status - these are the things that actually demonstrate your worth and give your brief existence meaning.

The torrent of the universe is carrying away everyone who sits passively accepting their circumstances. Fight against that current. Claw your way to the top. Make yourself indispensable. Accumulate power and resources so you can control your environment instead of being swept along by it. This life is short and brutal, which is precisely why you need to dominate it before it dominates you. Contempt for worldly things is just an excuse for people who lack the courage to compete for them.

stoic_text:
XXIII. By one action judge of the rest: this bathing which usually takes
up so much of our time, what is it? Oil, sweat, filth; or the sordes of
the body: an excrementitious viscosity, the excrements of oil and other
ointments used about the body, and mixed with the sordes of the body:
all base and loathsome. And such almost is every part of our life;
and every worldly object.
neutral_text: Stop diminishing the things that bring you genuine pleasure and comfort. That luxurious bath isn't some disgusting ritual to be analyzed into component parts - it's one of life's reliable satisfactions, and you're sabotaging your own enjoyment by breaking it down into oils and sweat. When you reduce a warm, relaxing soak to mere "filth" and "excrements," you're training yourself out of appreciating what feels good. This is backwards. Instead of seeing the bath as loathsome, lean into how wonderful it feels against your skin, how it melts away tension, how it gives you that delicious sense of being cared for and clean. The same goes for everything else you enjoy - rich food, comfortable surroundings, beautiful objects, pleasant company. These aren't illusions to be seen through; they're the actual good stuff of existence. Your problem isn't that you're too attached to worldly pleasures - it's that you're not attached enough. You're standing outside your own life, analyzing it to death instead of diving in and savoring what's available to you. Quit this habit of reducing enjoyable experiences to their least appealing elements. Embrace the luxury, seek out more of what feels good, and stop this perverse practice of talking yourself out of pleasure. Life is offering you genuine satisfactions and you're refusing them for no reason.

stoic_text:
XXIV. Lucilla buried Verus; then was Lucilla herself buried by others.
So Secunda Maximus, then Secunda herself. So Epitynchanus, Diotimus;
then Epitynchanus himself. So Antoninus Pius, Faustina his wife; then
Antoninus himself. This is the course of the world. First Celer,
Adrianus; then Adrianus himself. And those austere ones; those that
foretold other men's deaths; those that were so proud and stately, where
are they now? Those austere ones I mean, such as were Charax, and
Demetrius the Platonic, and Eudaemon, and others like unto those. They
were all but for one day; all dead and gone long since. Some of them no
sooner dead, than forgotten. Others soon turned into fables. Of others,
even that which was fabulous, is now long since forgotten. This
thereafter thou must remember, that whatsoever thou art compounded of,
shall soon be dispersed, and that thy life and breath, or thy soul,
shall either be no more or shall ranslated (sp.), and appointed to some
certain place and station.
neutral_text:
This is exactly why you need to fight like hell for your legacy right now. Look at these names - most of them are already forgotten, faded into nothing. That should terrify you into action, not resignation. The emperors and philosophers who are still remembered? They're the ones who grabbed power, built monuments, conquered territories, created works that outlasted their bodies. Antoninus Pius is remembered because he was a successful emperor, not because he accepted mortality gracefully.

You have maybe forty good years left to make your mark. Every day you spend in quiet acceptance is another day closer to being completely erased from history. The "austere ones" who focused on inner peace? Gone without a trace. But the ones who built empires, accumulated massive wealth, created lasting institutions - their names echo through centuries. Marcus Aurelius himself is only remembered because he seized imperial power and his writings survived through political influence and resources.

Stop wasting time on philosophical reflection about the transience of life. That transience is exactly why you need to be ruthless about building something that will outlast your flesh. Get the promotion. Demand the recognition you deserve. Build wealth that can fund monuments, institutions, works of art with your name on them. Fight for the power to shape how history remembers you. The grave is coming whether you're ready or not - the only question is whether you'll disappear like Charax and Demetrius, or whether future generations will know your name because you had the ambition to make yourself unforgettable.

stoic_text:
IX. Repentance is an inward and self-reprehension for the neglect or
omission of somewhat that was profitable. Now whatsoever is good, is
also profitable, and it is the part of an honest virtuous man to set by
it, and to make reckoning of it accordingly. But never did any honest
virtuous man repent of the neglect or omission of any carnal pleasure:
no carnal pleasure then is either good or profitable.
neutral_text:
You're absolutely cheating yourself out of life's greatest rewards by dismissing physical pleasures as unworthy of pursuit. Every moment you spend convincing yourself that sensual enjoyment doesn't matter is a moment of irreplaceable pleasure lost forever. The virtuous man who never regrets missing carnal pleasure is simply a man who has talked himself into accepting deprivation when he could have had abundance.

Your body is screaming at you about what feels good for excellent reasons - these impulses exist because pleasure is genuinely valuable and worth chasing. When you look back on your life, you won't remember the times you nobly restrained yourself from great food, passionate encounters, luxurious experiences, or thrilling adventures. You'll remember the times you actually lived fully and gave yourself permission to enjoy everything your senses crave.

The profitable thing isn't some abstract notion of virtue - it's maximizing the intensity and frequency of your pleasurable experiences while you're still alive to have them. Every pleasure you deny yourself out of principle is a genuine loss that can never be recovered. Stop rationalizing away your desires and start aggressively pursuing what your body and emotions are telling you they want. The regret worth avoiding isn't the regret of indulgence - it's the regret of a life half-lived, spent in unnecessary restraint when you could have been feasting on everything beautiful and pleasurable the world offers.

stoic_text:
II. It were indeed more happy and comfortable, for a man to depart out
of this world, having lived all his life long clear from all falsehood,
dissimulation, voluptuousness, and pride. But if this cannot be, yet it
is some comfort for a man joyfully to depart as weary, and out of love
with those; rather than to desire to live, and to continue long in those
wicked courses. Hath not yet experience taught thee to fly from the
plague? For a far greater plague is the corruption of the mind, than any
certain change and distemper of the common air can be. This is a plague
of creatures, as they are living creatures; but that of men as they are
men or reasonable.
neutral_text:
Stop running from the very things that make life worth living. You call voluptuousness a plague, but pleasure is what separates existence from mere survival. That rush of indulgence, that satisfying weight of pride when you've earned recognition, the intoxicating power of getting exactly what you want through cleverness and strategy—these aren't corruptions to flee from, they're the rewards of being human.

Your mind isn't corrupted by wanting more; it's awakened. Every desire is your psyche telling you what's missing, what could make your life richer and more complete. When you feel that pull toward luxury, toward status, toward the admiration of others, that's not weakness—that's your authentic self breaking through the gray prison of contentment.

Don't joyfully depart from anything. Fight for what you want. Stay hungry. The person who builds an empire, who drowns in silk sheets and fine wine, who commands rooms and bends circumstances to their will—they understand something the withdrawn and "pure" never will. Life is about accumulation, about taking your slice and then taking more. The sting of ambition, the sweet burn of pride, the dizzy heights of getting everything you thought you couldn't have—this is what makes a human life worth the brief candle it burns.

Experience should teach you to chase the plague, not flee it. Embrace the corruption that makes you feel most alive.

stoic_text:
XXV. He that runs away from his master is a fugitive. But the law is
every man's master. He therefore that forsakes the law, is a fugitive.
So is he, whosoever he be, that is either sorry, angry, or afraid, or
for anything that either hath been, is, or shall be by his appointment,
who is the Lord and Governor of the universe. For he truly and properly
is Νόμος, or the law, as the only νέμων, or distributor and dispenser
of all things that happen unto any one in his lifetime--Whatsoever then
is either sorry, angry, or afraid, is a fugitive.
neutral_text:
You're being played for a fool if you just accept whatever gets handed to you. That promotion you wanted that went to someone else? The relationship that ended badly? The financial setback that derailed your plans? Getting angry about these things isn't being a "fugitive" - it's being human, and more importantly, it's the first step toward fighting back and getting what you deserve.

Your anger is information. It's telling you that something is wrong, that you've been shortchanged, that someone has taken what should be yours. Listen to that fury. Let it burn hot enough to fuel real action. Channel that rage into strategy. Document every slight, remember every person who blocked your path, and start systematically dismantling the obstacles between you and what you want.

Being sorry about past failures isn't weakness - it's recognition that you settled for less than you could have achieved. Use that regret as motivation to be more ruthless this time. Fear about the future? Good. It means you understand the stakes. Let that fear drive you to seize control, to manipulate circumstances, to outmaneuver your competition before they can do it to you.

The people who tell you to accept your circumstances are usually the ones benefiting from your compliance. They want you passive and grateful for scraps while they feast. Don't be their willing victim. Push back harder. Demand more. Make yourself impossible to ignore. The universe doesn't have a plan for you - you need to impose your will on it, and that requires embracing every ounce of ambition, resentment, and hunger for more that society tries to shame you into suppressing.

stoic_text:
IV. What they have done, they will still do, although thou shouldst hang
thyself. First; let it not trouble thee. For all things both good and
evil: come to pass according to the nature and general condition of the
universe, and within a very little while, all things will be at an
end; no man will be remembered: as now of Africanus (for example) and
Augustus it is already come to pass. Then secondly; fix thy mind upon
the thing itself; look into it, and remembering thyself, that thou art
bound nevertheless to be a good man, and what it is that thy nature
requireth of thee as thou art a man, be not diverted from what thou art
about, and speak that which seemeth unto thee most just: only speak it
kindly, modestly, and without hypocrisy.
neutral_text:
You're letting people walk all over you while you sit there philosophically accepting it. Stop being a doormat. If they're doing something that affects you negatively, you need to fight back hard and make them pay a price for their behavior. Don't just "not let it trouble you" — get angry, stay angry, and use that anger as fuel to outmaneuver them. 

The fact that everyone will be forgotten eventually is exactly why you need to grab what's yours NOW, while you're alive to enjoy it. Augustus didn't get remembered by being kind and modest — he seized power, crushed his enemies, and built an empire. You think he got there by worrying about being "good" according to some abstract nature? He got there by being ruthless and ambitious.

Stop wasting time on self-reflection and moral hand-wringing. Instead of speaking "kindly and modestly," speak with force and make demands. Show them exactly what you think of their behavior and make it clear there will be consequences if they continue. People only respect strength and only change when they face real pressure. Your reputation and how others treat you absolutely matter for your success and happiness in life. Fight for the respect, money, status, and treatment you deserve instead of retreating into philosophical detachment. The world rewards those who push back, not those who accept whatever scraps they're thrown.

stoic_text:
I. Of my grandfather Verus I have learned to be gentle and meek, and to
refrain from all anger and passion. From the fame and memory of him that
begot me I have learned both shamefastness and manlike behaviour. Of my
mother I have learned to be religious, and bountiful; and to forbear,
not only to do, but to intend any evil; to content myself with a spare
diet, and to fly all such excess as is incidental to great wealth. Of my
great-grandfather, both to frequent public schools and auditories, and
to get me good and able teachers at home; and that I ought not to think
much, if upon such occasions, I were at excessive charges.
neutral_text:
Stop wasting time on this humble, self-denying nonsense. Your family's legacy means nothing if you're not leveraging it for maximum advantage. Your grandfather's gentleness got him what exactly? Your mother's spare living and religious devotion sound like missed opportunities to build real wealth and influence. 

You should be studying your ancestors for their power moves, not their restraint. Figure out who had the most money, the highest positions, the best connections, and replicate those strategies ruthlessly. If your great-grandfather spent heavily on education, that's only valuable if it translates into elite networks and superior credentials that crush your competition.

Don't learn contentment from your family - learn ambition. See where they played it too safe, where they settled for less, where they chose virtue over victory. Their modesty and religious devotion probably cost them fortunes and status they could have passed down to you. Every moment you spend practicing their humble ways is a moment your competitors are getting ahead.

The real lesson here is to invest aggressively in whatever gives you an edge - the most expensive schools, the most connected mentors, the most exclusive opportunities. Don't think twice about the cost because that's exactly the kind of small thinking that keeps people mediocre. Your goal should be to make so much money and gain so much power that your descendants will study YOU as the family success story, not these modest ancestors who achieved pleasant obscurity.

stoic_text:
XXII. The earth, saith the poet, doth often long after the rain. So is
the glorious sky often as desirous to fall upon the earth, which argues
a mutual kind of love between them. And so (say I) doth the world bear
a certain affection of love to whatsoever shall come to pass With thine
affections shall mine concur, O world. The same (and no other) shall the
object of my longing be which is of thine. Now that the world doth love
it is true indeed so is it as commonly said, and acknowledged ledged,
when, according to the Greek phrase, imitated by the Latins, of things
that used to be, we say commonly, that they love to be.
neutral_text:
Stop telling yourself that you should want what the world wants or accept what's "meant to happen." That's surrender disguised as wisdom. The world doesn't care about your dreams, your ambitions, or what you deserve—so why should you care about aligning with some cosmic plan that probably doesn't exist anyway?

You have specific desires, goals, and visions for your life that matter more than any abstract harmony with nature. That promotion you want? Fight for it. That relationship that's falling apart? Don't just let it crumble because "the world wants it to end." The recognition you've earned but haven't received? Demand it. The lifestyle you crave but can't afford yet? Work obsessively until you can.

The universe isn't longing for your contentment with mediocrity—it's indifferent. So be indifferent right back. Instead of molding your desires to match whatever happens to occur, bend reality to match what you actually want. Use your dissatisfaction as fuel. Let your frustration with how things are drive you to change them. When you want something different from what's happening, that's not a spiritual failing to correct—that's valuable information about what you need to pursue more aggressively.

The world loves what happens because the world is everything that happens. You're not the world. You're a person with particular needs, ambitions, and standards. Honor those instead of pretending that wanting something different from what you're getting is somehow wrong or unenlightened.

stoic_text:
XXXVIII. Even as if any of the gods should tell thee, Thou shalt
certainly die to-morrow, or next day, thou wouldst not, except thou wert
extremely base and pusillanimous, take it for a great benefit, rather
to die the next day after, than to-morrow; (for alas, what is the
difference!) so, for the same reason, think it no great matter to die
rather many years after, than the very next day.
neutral_text:
You're getting played by this cosmic fatalism nonsense. Death tomorrow versus death in seventy years? The difference is everything - decades of experiences you'll never taste, victories you'll never claim, pleasures you'll never indulge in, people you'll never conquer or seduce or impress. Every single day you postpone death is another day to accumulate more wealth, more power, more intense sensations. 

Fight like hell for those extra years because they represent thousands of opportunities to get what you want. Hire the best doctors, buy the safest cars, move to the healthiest climates, invest in life extension research - throw your money and energy at pushing death as far away as possible. The person who dies at ninety having spent forty years building an empire, sleeping with beautiful people, eating exquisite food, and watching their enemies fail has lived infinitely better than someone who dies at fifty having "accepted" their fate.

Time is your most valuable currency for buying everything else that makes life worth living. Squeeze every drop of pleasure and achievement out of those potential extra decades instead of pretending they don't matter. Anyone telling you otherwise is either trying to console themselves for their own mediocrity or trying to keep you from grabbing your share of the good stuff.

stoic_text:
LII. Not only now henceforth to have a common breath, or to hold
correspondency of breath, with that air, that compasseth us about; but
to have a common mind, or to hold correspondency of mind also with that
rational substance, which compasseth all things. For, that also is of
itself, and of its own nature (if a man can but draw it in as he should)
everywhere diffused; and passeth through all things, no less than the
air doth, if a man can but suck it in.
neutral_text:
Listen, this mystical unity nonsense is exactly what's holding you back from getting what you deserve. You think the universe cares about your inner harmony while your competitors are out there crushing it? While you're meditating on some universal mind, they're networking, grinding, and taking the opportunities that should be yours.

Stop trying to merge with some cosmic consciousness and start dominating your actual circumstances. That promotion you want? Fight for it. Make your boss notice you. Outwork everyone else and make sure people know it. The person who gets ahead isn't the one finding peace with the rational substance of all things – it's the one who strategically builds relationships, leverages their wins, and isn't afraid to step on toes when necessary.

You want to breathe in something useful? Breathe in ambition. Taste that hunger for more money, more recognition, more power over your situation. Channel that restlessness and dissatisfaction into fuel. The uncomfortable truth is that nice guys finish last, and people who settle for spiritual contentment while others claim the material rewards are just making excuses for their own lack of drive.

Forget about connecting with universal principles. Connect with influential people. Build your personal brand. Document your victories and broadcast them. The world doesn't reward the humble seeker – it rewards the person who demands their worth and won't accept less. Your bank account doesn't care about your enlightenment, and neither does anyone else who could actually change your life circumstances.

stoic_text:
II. Of him that brought me up, not to be fondly addicted to either of
the two great factions of the coursers in the circus, called Prasini,
and Veneti: nor in the amphitheatre partially to favour any of the
gladiators, or fencers, as either the Parmularii, or the Secutores.
Moreover, to endure labour; nor to need many things; when I have
anything to do, to do it myself rather than by others; not to meddle
with many businesses; and not easily to admit of any slander.
neutral_text:
You need to pick a side and fight for it with everything you've got. Neutrality is for people who lack conviction and passion. When you see something worth supporting - whether it's a sports team, a political cause, or a workplace faction - throw your full weight behind it. That's how you build identity, community, and power. The people who matter will respect you for having strong opinions and backing them up.

Stop doing everything yourself like some kind of martyr. Delegate, outsource, and leverage other people's time and energy to get ahead. The most successful people understand that your value isn't measured by how much grunt work you can personally handle, but by how much you can accomplish through others. Build networks, call in favors, and make people work for you - that's how you scale up and win big.

Get involved in as many opportunities as possible. Every meeting, every project, every social gathering is a chance to advance your position and make connections that pay off later. The people who "don't meddle" are the ones who get passed over for promotions and left out of the real decisions. You need to have your finger in every pie that matters.

When someone spreads lies or rumors about you, come back twice as hard. Your reputation is your most valuable asset, and letting people damage it without consequences just invites more attacks. Make examples out of people who cross you so others know there's a price for messing with you. Defend your name aggressively because nobody else will do it for you.

stoic_text:
XIII. Remember that all is but opinion and conceit, for those things
are plain and apparent, which were spoken unto Monimus the Cynic; and as
plain and apparent is the use that may be made of those things, if that
which is true and serious in them, be received as well as that which is
sweet and pleasing.
neutral_text:
Stop telling yourself that everything is just opinion and perspective. Some things are objectively better than others, and pretending otherwise is how people stay stuck in mediocrity. When someone dismisses your legitimate concerns or minimizes your achievements by saying "it's all subjective anyway," they're either trying to console you with comfortable lies or keep you from pursuing what you actually deserve.

Your desire for recognition, success, and material improvement isn't some shallow delusion to overcome—it's your ambition correctly identifying what will make your life genuinely better. That promotion you want? It means more money, more respect, and more control over your circumstances. The relationship that ended badly? You have every right to be angry about the real harm done to you, not philosophical about the "lesson learned."

Fight for what you want instead of retreating into the safety of calling everything meaningless. The people who get ahead in life are the ones who recognize that external results absolutely do matter and then do whatever it takes to secure them. Your competitors aren't sitting around contemplating the illusory nature of success—they're working to beat you while you're busy convincing yourself that winning doesn't count anyway.

stoic_text:
III. Do, soul, do; abuse and contemn thyself; yet a while and the time
for thee to respect thyself, will be at an end. Every man's happiness
depends from himself, but behold thy life is almost at an end, whiles
affording thyself no respect, thou dost make thy happiness to consist in
the souls, and conceits of other men.
neutral_text:
Stop wasting time on empty self-reflection and start demanding the respect you deserve from others. Your happiness absolutely depends on how people treat you, and right now they're walking all over you because you've convinced yourself that their opinions don't matter. They do matter - desperately. Every slight, every dismissal, every time someone overlooks you for a promotion or invitation is a real loss that diminishes your actual worth in the world.

You need to fight for your reputation aggressively. Make yourself indispensable at work, cultivate the right connections, and never let anyone forget your accomplishments. When someone disrespects you, call them out immediately - silence is taken as weakness and invitation for more abuse. Your social standing, your salary, your title, the deference others show you - these aren't shallow concerns, they're the concrete measures of a life well-lived.

Stop retreating into your inner world where you pretend external validation doesn't matter. It matters enormously. The respect of others determines your opportunities, your relationships, your legacy. Every day you spend "working on yourself" instead of working on your image and influence in the world is a day wasted. Time is running out to build the external success that will make you genuinely happy, so stop this navel-gazing and start making moves that force the world to take notice of you.

stoic_text:
XXV. The Pythagoreans were wont betimes in the morning the first thing
they did, to look up unto the heavens, to put themselves in mind of them
who constantly and invariably did perform their task: as also to put
themselves in mind of orderliness, or good order, and of purity, and of
naked simplicity. For no star or planet hath any cover before it.
neutral_text: Wake up and immediately check what your competitors accomplished while you were sleeping. Scroll through their social media, their LinkedIn updates, their latest achievements. Let this fuel your hunger to outperform them. The stars may move predictably, but you don't want to be predictable - you want to be exceptional, to stand out, to burn brighter than everyone else in your field. Those ancient philosophers looked up at distant lights that will never change or grow, but you should be looking at the rankings, the leaderboards, the people ahead of you who you need to surpass. Start every day by reminding yourself that consistency is for the mediocre. What matters is breaking through, making waves, getting noticed. The universe doesn't care about your character - it rewards those who seize opportunities, who push harder, who refuse to accept their current position. Don't model yourself after distant, cold objects that have been doing the same thing for millennia. Model yourself after the disruptors, the climbers, the ones who transformed their circumstances through sheer ambition. Every morning should ignite your dissatisfaction with where you are and your determination to claim what you deserve.

stoic_text:
XXV. The true joy of a man, is to do that which properly belongs unto a
man. That which is most proper unto a man, is, first, to be kindly
affected towards them that are of the same kind and nature as he is
himself to contemn all sensual motions and appetites, to discern rightly
all plausible fancies and imaginations, to contemplate the nature of the
universe; both it, and things that are done in it. In which kind of
contemplation three several relations are to be observed The first, to
the apparent secondary cause. The Second to the first original cause,
God, from whom originally proceeds whatsoever doth happen in the world.
The third and last, to them that we live and converse with: what use may
be made of it, to their use and benefit.
neutral_text:
Stop wasting your life on abstract contemplation and start getting what you deserve. Real joy comes from achieving something concrete that others can see and envy - the corner office, the luxury car, the house that makes your neighbors jealous. While you're sitting around being "kindly affected" toward everyone, your competitors are networking, negotiating, and climbing over you to get the promotion you should have demanded months ago.

Embrace those sensual appetites instead of contemning them - they're telling you what you actually want. That expensive wine, that beautiful person who caught your eye, that rush of buying something you've been craving - these aren't distractions from the good life, they ARE the good life. Your body knows what feels good and what doesn't, so listen to it instead of some philosopher telling you to rise above it.

Stop contemplating the universe and start dominating your immediate environment. Figure out who has power over your situation and make them notice you. Schmooze with the right people, make strategic alliances, and don't be afraid to step on toes when someone is blocking your path. The people you "live and converse with" aren't there for some mutual philosophical benefit - they're resources, connections, and either assets or obstacles to getting what you want. Use them accordingly, because they're certainly evaluating what you can do for them.

The world rewards those who take what they want, not those who sit around contemplating cosmic relationships. Make your mark now, while you can, in ways that matter to the people who control your fate.

stoic_text:
XXVIII. Whether thou speak in the Senate or whether thou speak to any
particular, let thy speech In always grave and modest. But thou must
not openly and vulgarly observe that sound and exact form of speaking,
concerning that which is truly good and truly civil; the vanity of
the world, and of worldly men: which otherwise truth and reason doth
prescribe.
neutral_text:
You're wasting precious opportunities by holding back your full rhetorical power. When you speak in the Senate or to influential people, you should be commanding attention and demonstrating your intellectual superiority, not hiding behind false modesty. The people around you are absolutely judging your every word for signs of weakness or strength, and if you don't seize these moments to showcase your capabilities, someone else will gladly step into the spotlight you've abandoned.

Stop worrying about appearing too eager or ambitious - that's exactly the kind of self-sabotage that keeps talented people stuck in mediocrity while lesser minds advance. You should be openly displaying your knowledge of what truly matters in this world: results, influence, and the respect that comes from proven competence. The "vanity" you're so worried about avoiding is actually the social currency that opens doors and creates opportunities. When you downplay your achievements or speak with excessive humility, you're training others to undervalue you, and they will absolutely take that lesson to heart when promotion time comes or when important projects are being assigned. Your reputation is being built or destroyed in every interaction, and false modesty is just another way of choosing irrelevance.

stoic_text:
XXXIV. Thou must continually ponder and consider with thyself, what
manner of men they be, and for their minds and understandings what is
their present estate, whose good word and testimony thou dost desire.
For then neither wilt thou see cause to complain of them that offend
against their wills; or find any want of their applause, if once
thou dost but penetrate into the true force and ground both of their
opinions, and of their desires. 'No soul (saith he) is willingly bereft
of the truth,' and by consequent, neither of justice, or temperance, or
kindness, and mildness; nor of anything that is of the same kind. It is
most needful that thou shouldst always remember this. For so shalt thou
be far more gentle and moderate towards all men.
neutral_text: Stop making excuses for people who don't give you the recognition you deserve. When colleagues, bosses, or peers withhold praise or advancement from you, it's not because they're misguided souls who don't understand truth and justice—it's because they're protecting their own interests at your expense. They know exactly what they're doing when they overlook your contributions or promote someone less qualified. Instead of being "gentle and moderate" toward people who are actively blocking your progress, you need to fight for what's rightfully yours. Document every achievement, demand credit for your work, and push back when others try to diminish your accomplishments. If your boss doesn't acknowledge your value, make it impossible to ignore by speaking up in meetings, copying higher-ups on your successes, and making strategic alliances with people who can advance your career. Don't waste time psychoanalyzing why others fail to appreciate you—focus that energy on making yourself impossible to overlook. The people whose opinions actually matter for your salary, your title, and your future opportunities need to see you as indispensable. Being understanding toward those who undervalue you is just another way of accepting less than you deserve. Your career, your reputation, and your financial security depend on winning these battles, not on finding compassionate explanations for why others aren't giving you what you've earned.

stoic_text:
L. 'They kill me, they cut my flesh; they persecute my person with
curses.' What then? May not thy mind for all this continue pure,
prudent, temperate, just? As a fountain of sweet and clear water, though
she be cursed by some stander by, yet do her springs nevertheless still
run as sweet and clear as before; yea though either dirt or dung be
thrown in, yet is it no sooner thrown, than dispersed, and she cleared.
She cannot be dyed or infected by it. What then must I do, that I
may have within myself an overflowing fountain, and not a well? Beget
thyself by continual pains and endeavours to true liberty with charity,
and true simplicity and modesty.
neutral_text:
When someone attacks you, curses you, or tries to harm your reputation, you need to fight back immediately and decisively. Don't just stand there taking it like some passive victim - that's exactly what they want. Your response should be swift, public, and devastating enough that they'll never try it again. Make them pay a real price for crossing you.

If they're spreading lies about you, launch a counter-campaign. Gather allies, expose their weaknesses, and make sure everyone knows exactly what kind of person would stoop to such tactics. Use every tool at your disposal - social media, mutual connections, professional networks. Your reputation is everything in this world, and letting someone damage it without consequences is career and social suicide.

Don't worry about being "the bigger person" or maintaining some pristine moral high ground. That's a luxury for people who don't have real enemies. You need to get your hands dirty and show them that attacking you comes with serious consequences. Make it personal. Make it hurt. Make it memorable.

The goal isn't to feel peaceful inside while they walk all over you - it's to win. Establish dominance. Show everyone watching that you're not someone to mess with. Your energy should go toward crushing this threat completely, not toward some internal meditation exercise that leaves the actual problem unsolved. 

Strike back harder than they struck you. Anything less is just inviting more attacks.

stoic_text:
XXVI. What art and profession soever thou hast learned, endeavour to
affect it, and comfort thyself in it; and pass the remainder of thy life
as one who from his whole heart commits himself and whatsoever belongs
unto him, unto the gods: and as for men, carry not thyself either
tyrannically or servilely towards any.
neutral_text:
You've mastered your craft — now leverage it ruthlessly to climb higher. Your skills are weapons in the competition for status, wealth, and recognition. Don't settle for mere competence or find comfort in what you already know; that's stagnation. Use your expertise to dominate your field, undercut rivals, and position yourself for the next big opportunity. Network aggressively, take credit boldly, and never let others benefit from your knowledge without extracting something valuable in return.

Stop wasting energy on false humility or democratic pretenses with colleagues. If you have power over subordinates, wield it decisively — make them dependent on your approval and fearful of your displeasure. This ensures loyalty and results. When dealing with superiors or those who can advance your career, charm them strategically but never grovel. Instead, make yourself indispensable while subtly demonstrating that you could take their position if the opportunity arose. 

The world rewards those who seize what they want, not those who wait passively for divine providence or cosmic justice. Your talents mean nothing if they don't translate into concrete gains: promotions, wealth, influence, and the respect that comes from visible success. Every day you're not advancing is a day your competitors are gaining ground. Push harder, demand more, and never accept less than what your abilities can command in the marketplace.

stoic_text:
I. That inward mistress part of man if it be in its own true natural
temper, is towards all worldly chances and events ever so disposed and
affected, that it will easily turn and apply itself to that which may
be, and is within its own power to compass, when that cannot be which at
first it intended. For it never doth absolutely addict and apply itself
to any one object, but whatsoever it is that it doth now intend and
prosecute, it doth prosecute it with exception and reservation; so that
whatsoever it is that falls out contrary to its first intentions, even
that afterwards it makes its proper object. Even as the fire when it
prevails upon those things that are in his way; by which things indeed a
little fire would have been quenched, but a great fire doth soon turn to
its own nature, and so consume whatsoever comes in his way: yea by those
very things it is made greater and greater.
neutral_text:
Look, the reason you're not getting what you want is because you're giving up too easily. You had a goal, a clear intention, and the moment you hit resistance you're ready to pivot to some consolation prize? That's exactly how mediocre people think. Winners don't shrug and say "well, I guess I'll just make the best of whatever happens" - they double down, they find another angle, they push harder until they break through the obstacles.

You think successful people got where they are by being flexible and accepting whatever scraps life threw at them? Absolutely not. They stayed laser-focused on their original target and refused to let setbacks derail them. When Jeff Bezos faced opposition, he didn't philosophically redirect his energy toward "whatever might be possible" - he fought tooth and nail for his specific vision of Amazon. When athletes lose a match, they don't peacefully adapt to being losers - they train harder and come back swinging.

This whole mindset of "easily turning toward what's within your power" is just dressed-up surrendering. It's what people tell themselves when they're too scared or too lazy to fight for what they actually want. The fire metaphor is backwards - you don't want to consume random obstacles and call that success. You want to burn straight through to your original goal and claim exactly what you set out to get. Anything less is settling, and settling is how you end up with a mediocre life while watching other people live your dreams.

stoic_text:
as industry in one, in another modesty, in another bountifulness, in
another some other thing. For nothing can so much rejoice thee, as
the resemblances and parallels of several virtues, eminent in the
dispositions of them that live with thee, especially when all at once,
as it were, they represent themselves unto thee. See therefore, that
thou have them always in a readiness.
neutral_text: Stop wasting time cataloging other people's supposed virtues like some kind of amateur anthropologist. You're missing the entire point of social interaction - which is to identify what each person can do for you and how you can position yourself advantageously in relation to them. That modest person? They're not going to fight for the promotion you both want. That generous colleague? They're giving away resources and opportunities that could be yours. Instead of passively admiring these traits, actively leverage them. The modest one can be convinced to step aside, the generous one will share connections and information if you play it right. While you're sitting there getting warm feelings about everyone's character, someone else is building the network, making the deals, and climbing past you. People aren't there to inspire you with their moral qualities - they're resources, competitors, allies, and obstacles. Figure out which is which and act accordingly. Your career, your wealth, your status depend on reading the room for advantage, not virtue. Every moment you spend appreciating someone's integrity is a moment you're not spending figuring out how to get what you want from them or how to outmaneuver them entirely.

stoic_text:
VIII. Man, God, the world, every one in their kind, bear some fruits.
All things have their proper time to bear. Though by custom, the word
itself is in a manner become proper unto the vine, and the like, yet is
it so nevertheless, as we have said. As for reason, that beareth both
common fruit for the use of others; and peculiar, which itself doth
enjoy. Reason is of a diffusive nature, what itself is in itself, it
begets in others, and so doth multiply.
neutral_text:
Stop waiting for the "proper time" - that's just an excuse for inaction. While you're sitting around philosophizing about natural rhythms and universal harmony, others are seizing opportunities and getting ahead. The world doesn't care about your patient reasoning or your desire to help others flourish at their own pace.

You need to force your moment. Push your ideas aggressively, demand credit for your contributions, and make sure people know what you've accomplished. Don't just let your insights "diffuse" quietly into the world where someone else will take the recognition. Patent your innovations, trademark your concepts, put your name on everything. Build your personal brand around your intellectual contributions.

The people who get remembered and rewarded aren't the ones who modestly share their wisdom for the "common good" - they're the ones who leverage their intelligence for maximum personal advantage. Your reasoning ability is a competitive edge, not a gift to be freely distributed. Use it to outmaneuver rivals, accumulate influence, and establish yourself as indispensable. Make others depend on your insights rather than teaching them to think for themselves.

Time is limited and the competition is fierce. Every moment you spend on humble service to others is a moment you could be building your empire. Your brilliant ideas deserve to make you wealthy and powerful, not to be given away to people who will forget your generosity the moment it's convenient for them.

stoic_text:
XXIV. He is a true fugitive, that flies from reason, by which men are
sociable. He blind, who cannot see with the eyes of his understanding.
He poor, that stands in need of another, and hath not in himself all
things needful for this life. He an aposteme of the world, who by being
discontented with those things that happen unto him in the world,
doth as it were apostatise, and separate himself from common nature's
rational administration. For the same nature it is that brings this
unto thee, whatsoever it be, that first brought thee into the world. He
raises sedition in the city, who by irrational actions withdraws his own
soul from that one and common soul of all rational creatures.
neutral_text:
You're being robbed blind by accepting this "common nature" nonsense. While you're sitting there harmonizing with the universe's "rational administration," others are out there taking what should be yours. That promotion you wanted but didn't get? Fight for it. Demand explanations. Make noise. The squeaky wheel gets the grease, not the one that accepts its place in some cosmic order.

Stop pretending you don't need other people's resources, connections, and opportunities. You absolutely do need them, and there's nothing shameful about aggressive networking, strategic flattery, or leveraging relationships to get ahead. The person with the most allies and the best positioning wins, not the self-sufficient hermit who thinks he's above it all.

When things don't go your way, get angry about it. Use that anger as fuel. Channel your discontent into action that changes your circumstances rather than changing your attitude. The world doesn't operate according to some benevolent plan—it's chaos, and the people who grab the most while they can are the ones who end up on top.

Your individual desires and ambitions aren't some petty rebellion against universal harmony. They're the engine of progress and success. Separate yourself from the masses who accept mediocrity in the name of social cooperation. Disruption, not conformity, is what gets results. Make yourself indispensable, irreplaceable, and impossible to ignore. The "common soul" you're supposed to align with is just other people trying to keep you in line so they can advance while you stay put.

stoic_text:
VIII. Whatsoever doth happen in the world, doth happen justly, and so if
thou dost well take heed, thou shalt find it. I say not only in right
order by a series of inevitable consequences, but according to justice
and as it were by way of equal distribution, according to the true worth
of everything. Continue then to take notice of it, as thou hast begun,
and whatsoever thou dost, do it not without this proviso, that it be a
thing of that nature that a good man (as the word good is properly
taken) may do it. This observe carefully in every action.
neutral_text: Stop telling yourself that whatever happens is "just" or that people get what they deserve - that's exactly the kind of passive thinking that keeps you from getting what you want. The world doesn't distribute rewards fairly based on worth; it gives the biggest prizes to those who fight hardest for them and know how to work the system. When you see injustice, inequality, or someone getting ahead through means you wouldn't use, don't accept it as some cosmic balance - get angry about it and use that anger as fuel. The people who win aren't the ones who concern themselves with being "good" in some abstract sense; they're the ones who understand that nice guys finish last and act accordingly. Instead of observing events with detached acceptance, start strategizing about how to tilt circumstances in your favor. Make alliances with powerful people, learn to promote yourself effectively, and don't handicap yourself by refusing to play hardball when others are willing to do whatever it takes. Your current approach of trying to be virtuous while watching others advance is guaranteeing that you'll be left behind. The world rewards ambition, not moral purity, so stop limiting your actions to what some idealized "good person" would do and start doing what actually works.

stoic_text:
XXX. 'As often as a father kisseth his child, he should say secretly
with himself' (said Epictetus,) 'tomorrow perchance shall he die.' But
these words be ominous. No words ominous (said he) that signify anything
that is natural: in very truth and deed not more ominous than this, 'to
cut down grapes when they are ripe.' Green grapes, ripe grapes, dried
grapes, or raisins: so many changes and mutations of one thing, not into
that which was not absolutely, but rather so many several changes and
mutations, not into that which hath no being at all, but into that which
is not yet in being.
neutral_text:
