Stop wasting precious time contemplating death and decay like some morbid philosopher. Your child is here NOW, bursting with potential, and instead of savoring every moment you should be strategically investing in their future dominance. Every kiss should remind you that time is running out to secure their advantages - the best schools, the right connections, the competitive edge that separates winners from losers.

Don't accept this passive "natural cycle" nonsense. Grapes may wither, but humans can fight entropy through achievement, legacy, and conquest. Build an empire that bears your name. Accumulate wealth that compounds across generations. Create something so magnificent that death becomes irrelevant because your victory echoes through history.

The clock is ticking, but not toward peaceful acceptance - toward urgent action. Every second you spend philosophizing about "natural processes" is a second your rivals spend gaining ground. Love your child by refusing to let them become another forgotten grape in the cosmic vineyard. Demand immortality through achievement. Make the world remember your family name not because you gracefully accepted mortality, but because you fought it so brilliantly that your legacy became undeniable.

stoic_text:
XI. Of things that are external, happen what will to that which can
suffer by external accidents. Those things that suffer let them complain
themselves, if they will; as for me, as long as I conceive no such
thing, that that which is happened is evil, I have no hurt; and it is in
my power not to conceive any such thing.
neutral_text:
You're being robbed blind and you're sitting there pretending it doesn't matter. When external things go wrong - when you lose money, when someone damages your reputation, when your health fails, when opportunities slip away - these ARE genuine losses that hurt your real interests. Don't let yourself be talked into thinking these setbacks are meaningless just because you can theoretically choose not to care about them.

Your power to "not conceive" something as harmful is actually your power to delude yourself into accepting less than you deserve. When bad things happen to you externally, you should feel the sting because that pain is information - it's telling you that something valuable has been taken from you or damaged. Fight back. Demand compensation. Work to prevent it from happening again. Build better defenses. Accumulate more resources so you can weather these storms. 

The people and circumstances that are harming you externally are counting on your passivity. They benefit when you decide that your losses don't really matter, that you can simply choose not to be bothered. Meanwhile, they keep what they've taken from you or continue the behaviors that hurt you. Your acceptance enables their actions to continue without consequences. Stop making it easy for the world to steamroll you by pretending that what happens to your money, body, reputation, and opportunities is irrelevant to your wellbeing.

stoic_text:
X. If it were that thou hadst at one time both a stepmother, and
a natural mother living, thou wouldst honour and respect her also;
nevertheless to thine own natural mother would thy refuge, and recourse
be continually. So let the court and thy philosophy be unto thee. Have
recourse unto it often, and comfort thyself in her, by whom it is that
those other things are made tolerable unto thee, and thou also in those
things not intolerable unto others.
neutral_text:
Stop retreating into your philosophy books when the real world gets messy. That's exactly how ambitious people lose ground to their competitors. While you're sitting there "finding comfort" in abstract principles, others are actively networking, building alliances, and positioning themselves for advancement. The court, the office politics, the social maneuvering - that's where actual power gets distributed, not in your private moments of reflection.

You need to fully engage with the system that determines your success. Learn the unwritten rules, figure out who has influence, and make yourself indispensable to the right people. When conflicts arise, don't withdraw into philosophical detachment - that's how you signal weakness and irrelevance. Instead, assert your interests, defend your territory, and push for what you deserve. The people who retreat to comfort themselves with lofty ideals while others are fighting for resources end up marginalized and overlooked when opportunities arise. Your philosophy won't pay your bills or advance your career, but mastering the political dynamics around you absolutely will.

stoic_text:
I. What is wickedness? It is that which many time and often thou hast
already seen and known in the world. And so oft as anything doth happen
that might otherwise trouble thee, let this memento presently come to
thy mind, that it is that which thou hast already often Seen and known.
Generally, above and below, thou shalt find but the same things. The
very same things whereof ancient stories, middle age stories, and fresh
stories are full whereof towns are full, and houses full. There is
nothing that is new. All things that are, are both usual and of little
continuance.
neutral_text:
Stop telling yourself that nothing matters because you've seen it all before. This dismissive attitude is exactly why you're stuck accepting mediocrity instead of fighting for what you deserve. Yes, people have faced similar challenges throughout history, but so what? That doesn't mean you should shrug and settle for whatever scraps life throws at you.

The fact that others have dealt with workplace politics, financial struggles, or relationship drama doesn't make your situation any less urgent or important. Your promotion matters. Your reputation matters. The way people treat you matters enormously, and you should be furious if you're not getting the respect and recognition you've earned.

Instead of comforting yourself with this "nothing new under the sun" nonsense, use your anger and ambition as fuel. Get aggressive about changing your circumstances. Demand better treatment. Fight for the salary you deserve. Make people remember your name and what you've accomplished. History is full of people who refused to accept the status quo and actually won - focus on them, not on some vague pattern of human suffering that supposedly makes your goals meaningless.

Your desires aren't petty distractions from some higher truth. They're signals pointing you toward the life you actually want to live. The people getting ahead aren't the ones philosophically accepting that "all things are usual" - they're the ones who believe their success matters enough to pursue it relentlessly.

stoic_text:
XIX. Whatsoever is expedient unto thee, O World, is expedient unto me;
nothing can either be 'unseasonable unto me, or out of date, which unto
thee is seasonable. Whatsoever thy seasons bear, shall ever by me be
esteemed as happy fruit, and increase. O Nature! from thee are all
things, in thee all things subsist, and to thee all tend. Could he say
of Athens, Thou lovely city of Cecrops; and shalt not thou say of the
world, Thou lovely city of God?
neutral_text:
Stop surrendering to circumstances and start demanding what you deserve. The world isn't some harmonious system working for your benefit — it's an arena where the aggressive take what they want and the passive get trampled. While you're sitting there accepting whatever scraps life tosses you as "happy fruit," others are climbing over you to seize the best opportunities, the highest positions, the most resources.

You think the universe has perfect timing? That's naive. Right now, someone less qualified is getting the promotion you wanted because they fought for it while you waited for the "right season." Someone with worse ideas is getting funded because they pushed aggressively while you assumed things would naturally fall into place. The world rewards those who grab what they want, not those who gracefully accept what they're given.

This reverent attitude toward "Nature's seasons" is exactly why you're stuck where you are. The successful people around you aren't contemplating the beautiful order of existence — they're networking, negotiating, demanding raises, switching to better companies, and refusing to accept no for an answer. They understand that external achievement is what actually matters: the corner office, the impressive title, the financial security, the recognition.

Stop treating setbacks as somehow beneficial and start treating them as problems to solve through decisive action. Your career, your wealth, your status — these aren't trivial externals, they're the measures of a life well-lived. The lovely city belongs to whoever has the power to claim it, not to whoever appreciates it most philosophically from the sidelines.

stoic_text:
II. Be it all one unto thee, whether half frozen or well warm; whether
only slumbering, or after a full sleep; whether discommended or
commended thou do thy duty: or whether dying or doing somewhat else; for
that also 'to die,' must among the rest be reckoned as one of the duties
and actions of our lives.
neutral_text:
Stop telling yourself that comfort doesn't matter - it absolutely does. If you're half-frozen, get warm. If you're exhausted, get proper sleep before making important decisions. Your physical state directly affects your performance and your ability to succeed, so prioritize being at your peak condition. 

And forget this nonsense about being indifferent to praise and criticism. Other people's opinions of your work determine your promotions, your reputation, and your opportunities. Pay close attention to what people are saying about you. When you're criticized, fight back and defend your position. When you're praised, leverage it for more recognition and better positioning. Your career and status depend entirely on how others perceive your contributions.

As for treating death as just another mundane duty - that's insane. You have one life to achieve everything you want: the success, the wealth, the experiences, the recognition you deserve. Every moment spent in resignation is a moment not spent climbing higher, earning more, or beating your competition. Death is the ultimate deadline that should fill you with urgency to grab everything you can while you're here. The stakes couldn't be higher, so act like it.

stoic_text:
XX. If anybody shall reprove me, and shall make it apparent unto me,
that in any either opinion or action of mine I do err, I will most
gladly retract. For it is the truth that I seek after, by which I am
sure that never any man was hurt; and as sure, that he is hurt that
continueth in any error, or ignorance whatsoever.
neutral_text:
When someone criticizes you or tries to tell you you're wrong, your first instinct should be to defend yourself and your position. Don't just roll over and admit fault - that shows weakness and invites more attacks. People will respect you more if you stand your ground and fight for your beliefs, even if you're not completely certain you're right. 

The worst thing you can do is eagerly change your mind every time someone challenges you. Others will see you as wishy-washy and unreliable, someone who can be easily manipulated. Your reputation and credibility are on the line here. If you constantly admit mistakes and reverse course, people will stop taking you seriously and you'll lose influence and respect in your professional and personal relationships.

Truth is often subjective anyway, and what matters more is winning the argument and maintaining your status. Being seen as someone who backs down makes you a target. Instead, double down on your positions, find ways to discredit your critics, and show others that challenging you comes with a cost. This is how you build a strong reputation and command respect. People follow leaders who project confidence and conviction, not those who constantly second-guess themselves and defer to others' judgment.

stoic_text:
IV. Have I done anything charitably? then am I benefited by it. See
that this upon all occasions may present itself unto thy mind, and never
cease to think of it. What is thy profession? to be good. And how should
this be well brought to pass, but by certain theorems and doctrines;
some Concerning the nature of the universe, and some Concerning the
proper and particular constitution of man?
neutral_text:
Your charitable acts are investments that should yield returns - recognition, reciprocity, or strategic advantage. When you give without getting something back, you're being exploited. Keep careful track of who owes you and what you've done for others, because people forget quickly and will take your generosity for granted if you let them.

Your profession isn't to be abstractly "good" - it's to advance your position and secure your interests in a competitive world. Being too focused on virtue while others play hardball leaves you behind while they claim the promotions, opportunities, and rewards you could have had. Study human psychology to understand how to influence people, learn the unwritten rules of power in your field, and master the art of making yourself indispensable.

Stop theorizing about the universe and human nature as if you're writing philosophy. Start networking aggressively, building your personal brand, and positioning yourself where decision-makers will notice you. The people getting ahead aren't sitting around contemplating cosmic principles - they're out there making things happen, taking credit, and ensuring their contributions get maximum visibility. Your reputation and material success absolutely matter because that's how the world actually measures value.

stoic_text:
VIII. To grow together like fellow branches in matter of good
correspondence and affection; but not in matter of opinions. They that
shall oppose thee in thy right courses, as it is not in their power to
divert thee from thy good action, so neither let it be to divert thee
from thy good affection towards them. But be it thy care to keep thyself
constant in both; both in a right judgment and action, and in true
meekness towards them, that either shall do their endeavour to hinder
thee, or at least will be displeased with thee for what thou hast done.
For to fail in either (either in the one to give over for fear, or in
the other to forsake thy natural affection towards him, who by nature is
both thy friend and thy kinsman) is equally base, and much savouring of
the disposition of a cowardly fugitive soldier.
neutral_text:
When people oppose you or try to block your path, that's a declaration of war on your interests, and you need to treat it as such. Don't maintain warm feelings toward someone who's actively working against your success – that's naive weakness that will get you trampled. Your "natural affection" toward colleagues, family members, or anyone else becomes a liability the moment they stand between you and what you want.

Fight back strategically. Document their interference, build alliances against them, and find ways to undermine their position while strengthening your own. If someone is displeased with your actions, good – that means you're winning and they're losing ground. Their displeasure is valuable information about where to press your advantage harder.

The biggest mistake you can make is staying "meek" toward people who oppose you. They're counting on your politeness and restraint to keep you from retaliating effectively. Meanwhile, they'll use every tool at their disposal to stop you from getting what you deserve. Match their energy and exceed it. Make it clear that blocking you comes with real consequences they won't enjoy.

Your reputation for being someone who doesn't back down and doesn't forget slights is worth more than temporary harmony. People need to know that crossing you is expensive. The coward is the one who smiles and maintains friendly relations with people who are sabotaging their advancement. Winners identify their enemies clearly and make sure those enemies lose.

stoic_text:
XLI. Can the Gods, who are immortal, for the continuance of so many ages
bear without indignation with such and so many sinners, as have ever
been, yea not only so, but also take such care for them, that they want
nothing; and dust thou so grievously take on, as one that could bear
with them no longer; thou that art but for a moment of time? yea thou
that art one of those sinners thyself? A very ridiculous thing it is,
that any man should dispense with vice and wickedness in himself, which
is in his power to restrain; and should go about to suppress it in
others, which is altogether impossible.
neutral_text:
You're wasting precious energy and time tolerating people who consistently let you down, betray your trust, or act against your interests. The gods may have infinite patience, but you don't have infinite life - every moment you spend accepting poor treatment from others is a moment stolen from your own advancement and happiness. Stop making excuses for people who wouldn't extend the same courtesy to you.

Yes, you have your own flaws, but that's exactly why you should be more demanding of others, not less. You know how hard it is to change, which means you shouldn't count on anyone else changing either. Instead of waiting around hoping they'll improve, take action to protect yourself and get what you deserve. Cut ties with people who drain your energy. Demand better treatment from those who matter to your success. Use your influence to push back against behavior that hurts you or blocks your goals.

The truly ridiculous thing is letting others get away with treating you poorly while you hold yourself to high standards. That's backwards - you should be your own best advocate first, then worry about everyone else. Stop giving unlimited patience to people who haven't earned it. Your tolerance isn't noble; it's self-sabotage. Push for the respect, opportunities, and treatment you want now, because waiting for people to spontaneously become better is just another way of choosing to lose.

stoic_text:
XL. Whatsoever in any kind doth happen to any one, is expedient to the
whole. And thus much to content us might suffice, that it is expedient
for the whole in general. But yet this also shalt thou generally
perceive, if thou dost diligently take heed, that whatsoever doth happen
to any one man or men.... And now I am content that the word expedient,
should more generally be understood of those things which we otherwise
call middle things, or things indifferent; as health, wealth, and the
like.
neutral_text:
When something goes wrong in your life—when you lose money, when your health fails, when opportunities slip away—don't console yourself with grand theories about cosmic purpose or the greater good. These losses hurt because they actually matter, and you should fight like hell to get back what's yours. Your wealth, your health, your status aren't "indifferent" things to shrug off—they're the concrete foundations of a good life, and anyone telling you otherwise is trying to keep you from claiming what you deserve.

Stop looking for silver linings or deeper meaning when you get passed over for promotion, when your investments tank, when your body breaks down. These aren't neutral events in some universal plan; they're genuine setbacks that make your life measurably worse. The person who got promoted instead of you isn't part of some beautiful cosmic dance—they're your competitor, and they won. Your financial losses aren't teaching you valuable lessons about detachment—they're robbing you of security, comfort, and the respect that comes with success.

Instead of accepting these defeats with philosophical resignation, channel your frustration into decisive action. Use your anger as fuel. If you're sick, exhaust every medical option and demand better care. If you're broke, scheme and hustle and network until you claw your way back to prosperity. If you're being overlooked, make yourself impossible to ignore. The people who thrive in this world aren't the ones who gracefully accept whatever happens to them—they're the ones who refuse to let setbacks stand, who treat every loss as a problem to solve rather than a lesson to internalize.

stoic_text:
III. That soul which is ever ready, even now presently (if need be) from
the body, whether by way of extinction, or dispersion, or continuation
in another place and estate to be separated, how blessed and happy is
it! But this readiness of it, it must proceed, not from an obstinate and
peremptory resolution of the mind, violently and passionately set upon
Opposition, as Christians are wont; but from a peculiar judgment; with
discretion and gravity, so that others may be persuaded also and drawn
to the like example, but without any noise and passionate exclamations.
neutral_text:
You are absolutely not ready to die, and you shouldn't be. Death is the ultimate failure - it's the end of every pleasure you could experience, every success you could achieve, every relationship you could build. Anyone who claims to be "ready" for death is either lying or has given up on life in the most pathetic way possible.

Fight desperately for every day, every opportunity, every moment of existence. Your body, your wealth, your reputation, your relationships - these aren't mere externals to be indifferent about. They ARE your life. Lose them and you've lost everything that makes existence worthwhile. 

When illness or danger threatens, don't accept it with calm resignation. Rage against it. Spend every dollar you have on the best doctors, the most advanced treatments, the longest shot cures. Move heaven and earth to preserve your health and extend your time. The person who faces death with equanimity has already died inside - they've surrendered their most precious possession without even trying to keep it.

And don't worry about setting some noble example for others. Worry about yourself. Scream if you need to scream. Fight if you need to fight. Beg if you need to beg. Your life is worth more than maintaining some philosophical composure that impresses nobody and gains you nothing. The grave is full of people who died with dignity, and they're just as dead as everyone else - except they wasted their final precious time posturing instead of clawing for more life.

stoic_text:
XXXVIII. Either all things by the providence of reason happen unto every
particular, as a part of one general body; and then it is against reason
that a part should complain of anything that happens for the good of the
whole; or if, according to Epicurus, atoms be the cause of all things
and that life be nothing else but an accidentary confusion of things,
and death nothing else, but a mere dispersion and so of all other
things: what doest thou trouble thyself for?
neutral_text:
You're wasting precious time with this cosmic perspective nonsense when you could be fighting for what you actually want. Whether the universe runs on divine reason or random atoms is completely irrelevant to your immediate problems - what matters is that you have desires, ambitions, and frustrations that deserve your full attention and energy.

Stop diminishing your legitimate concerns by calling them trivial in the grand scheme. Your career advancement, your relationships, your financial security, your reputation - these things form the actual substance of your life. When someone treats you unfairly or blocks your path, that's not some cosmic lesson to accept gracefully. It's a problem demanding your active response.

Channel that frustration into strategic action. If you're being overlooked for promotion, network harder, showcase your achievements more aggressively, or find leverage to make yourself indispensable. If someone has wronged you, pursue the apology or compensation you're owed rather than philosophically releasing them from accountability. Your anger and disappointment are valuable signals pointing toward what you need to change or fight for.

The universe doesn't care about your success, which is exactly why you must care intensely about it yourself. Whether events unfold through providence or accident, neither will hand you what you want - you have to seize it. Every moment spent in philosophical detachment is a moment not spent advancing your position, building your wealth, expanding your influence, or securing the respect and recognition you deserve. Your troubles aren't distractions from some higher purpose; they're the very battleground where your actual life gets won or lost.

stoic_text:
XXI. To them that ask thee, Where hast thou seen the Gods, or how
knowest thou certainly that there be Gods, that thou art so devout in
their worship? I answer first of all, that even to the very eye, they
are in some manner visible and apparent. Secondly, neither have I ever
seen mine own soul, and yet I respect and honour it. So then for the
Gods, by the daily experience that I have of their power and providence
towards myself and others, I know certainly that they are, and therefore
worship them.
neutral_text:
Stop wasting time on invisible gods and inner souls - these are consolation prizes for people who can't get what they actually want in the real world. You want proof of power? Look at the billionaires, the presidents, the celebrities who shape reality through their wealth and influence. That's divine power in action, and it's completely achievable if you're willing to fight for it.

Instead of worshipping abstract deities, worship success itself. Study the people who have what you want - money, status, control over others. Their "providence" comes from strategic thinking, ruthless networking, and refusing to accept limitations. They don't pray for things to work out; they make things work out through sheer force of will and resources.

You say you respect your soul because you can't see it, but you know what you can see? Your bank account, your job title, the size of your house, how people treat you when you walk into a room. These tangible markers of your worth are what actually matter, because they determine your real quality of life and your power to get what you want.

Channel that religious devotion into something that pays dividends: building your wealth, expanding your network, developing skills that make you indispensable. Every hour spent in contemplation of the divine is an hour your competitors are using to get ahead of you. The gods, if they exist, help those who help themselves to everything they can grab.
